This complements OpenAI's broader Microsoft-ecosystem play: Workspace Agents (released May 6 with Codex-powered enterprise pricing), the ChatGPT sidebar in Excel (now GA), and the existing ChatGPT Enterprise Salesforce/Slack/Atlassian connectors. ChatGPT for Intune is the missing piece for organizations that have standardized on Intune/Entra as their device-management spine — the federal government, large financial services, regulated healthcare. Without it, those orgs could not allow ChatGPT on managed mobile devices without policy waivers.

Cross-lab comparison: Anthropic's Claude iOS and Android apps have no equivalent Intune-managed variant. Google's Gemini app benefits from Google's own Workspace MDM but does not integrate with Intune. xAI's Grok has no managed-mobile story. Microsoft Copilot, of course, runs natively under Intune as a Microsoft product. OpenAI's move closes a competitive gap with Microsoft Copilot on managed-device deployment, which matters for the same Fortune 500 buyers Anthropic is courting through the Microsoft 365 add-ins. Watch for an Anthropic equivalent — Claude for Intune — in the Q3 2026 product roadmap.

Sources:

  • https://apps.apple.com/us/app/chatgpt-for-intune/id6759581694
  • https://9to5mac.com/2026/05/04/openai-releases-a-separate-chatgpt-ios-app-for-schools-and-work-organizations/
  • https://help.openai.com/en/articles/20001232-setting-up-chatgpt-for-intune

item.title: Anthropic Outcomes scoring lands May 6: separate grader, 10-point lift, 10.1% on PowerPoint, 8.4% on Word — process supervision quantified
item.description:
Folded into the same May 6 Managed Agents release as dreaming, Anthropic shipped Outcomes in public beta — a process-supervision feature where developers write a rubric describing what a successful task output looks like, and a separate grader Claude evaluates the agent's output against that rubric in its own context window. When the grader flags a problem, the agent gets a structured signal pointing to exactly what to revise. The agent then takes another pass, and the cycle repeats until the rubric is satisfied or a budget caps it.

The methodological care matters. The grader runs in a separate context window from the agent. This means the grader is not biased by the agent's chain of reasoning — it is judging the output against the rubric the way a fresh human reviewer would. That isolation is what produces the gains. Anthropic's reported numbers: roughly 10 percentage points of average improvement on agentic tasks, with two specific file-generation lifts called out: 8.4% on Word documents and 10.1% on PowerPoint.

Why the file-generation lifts are bigger: documents are exactly the kind of artifact where the agent's chain-of-thought correctness is decoupled from the final output quality. An agent can reason correctly about what should be in slide 4 and still produce slide 4 with broken bullets. A separate grader catches the artifact problem the reasoning trace did not flag.

The connection to broader research is direct. This is process supervision (Process Reward Model, PRM) shipped as a developer-facing feature. The field has been writing PRM papers for two years — OpenAI's "Let's Verify Step by Step," DeepMind's Process Reward work, the broader Math Shepherd literature. Anthropic's contribution here is taking PRM out of the training-time research lab and making it a runtime feature any developer using Managed Agents can configure. Write the rubric, get the lift.

Combined with dreaming (memory curation across sessions), outcomes makes Claude Managed Agents the most production-tested instance of the verify-then-iterate pattern shipped by any frontier lab. OpenAI's Workspace Agents do not expose an analogous rubric+grader feature externally; they have it internally but as a Codex-internal evaluation harness, not a developer surface. Google's ADK has agent-graph capabilities but no explicit rubric-grader feature. Mistral's Workflows are durable orchestration, not process supervision.

The likely competitive response: OpenAI exposes a developer-facing rubric feature in Workspace Agents within Q3, Google adds rubric grading to ADK at I/O May 19-20, and Mistral adds a Workflows grading task. The PRM-as-developer-feature category just opened.

Sources:

  • https://9to5mac.com/2026/05/07/anthropic-updates-claude-managed-agents-with-three-new-features/
  • https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/managed-agents/overview
